dataset_summary: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Replace this with your own paragraph. Safe facts: the modeling panel contains 5,025 country-year rows across 207 economies from 2000 to 2024, and the target is merchandise exports in current US$.
problem_importance: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Explain why forecasting export value matters in your own words. Tie it to trade planning, macro monitoring, or policy prioritization.
approach_findings: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Replace this with your own 1-2 paragraphs. Safe facts: the best held-out model is Linear Regression with RMSE $28,826,503,003, MAE $9,062,467,080, and R^2 0.991.

captions.target_distribution: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Exports are strongly right-skewed. Median exports are $5,126,000,000 and the maximum reaches $3,576,653,000,000.
captions.exports_by_region: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] North America has the highest median export level in the panel. Regional spread is wide, which suggests structural differences matter.
captions.gdp_vs_exports: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] GDP and exports have a correlation of 0.83 in the raw panel. The positive slope supports including macro scale variables in the models.
captions.lag_exports_vs_current: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Prior-year exports correlate with current exports at 0.99. That persistence should help both tree models and the MLP.
captions.imports_vs_exports: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Imports and exports correlate at 0.95, suggesting that more trade-connected economies tend to move both measures together.
captions.correlation_heatmap: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] The strongest off-diagonal correlation is between exports_usd and lag_1_exports_usd at 0.99.

model_comparison: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Compare the best model against the baseline and the MLP in your own words. Use the metrics table to support every claim.
shap_interpretation: [MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED] Start from this fact scaffold and rewrite it in your own words: the best tree-based model is Random Forest and its top SHAP feature is num__imports_usd.
